title: Sentry user authentication tokens should not be disclosed
description: Sentry is an error tracking and application performance monitoring (APM) platform that helps developers identify, diagnose, and fix bugs and performance issues in real-time. Sentry user authentication tokens (sntryu_ prefix) provide user-level API access to Sentry's platform, granting programmatic access to projects and resources that the specific user has permissions to access. These tokens enable user-specific operations including: accessing error data and stack traces from projects the user can view, creating and managing releases for accessible projects, configuring alert rules for user's projects, accessing issue histories and performance monitoring data, managing user's personal settings and preferences, creating and modifying issues and events, accessing project configurations and integrations the user manages, and performing API operations within the scope of the user's granted permissions. User authentication tokens are scoped to individual user accounts and respect the user's role-based access control (RBAC) permissions within the organization. Exposing Sentry user authentication tokens enables attackers to: access sensitive error data containing API keys, database credentials, authentication tokens, and personally identifiable information (PII) leaked through stack traces and context variables from all projects the user can access, manipulate or delete error tracking data to hide malicious activity or disrupt monitoring for accessible projects, access release information revealing application architecture and deployment patterns, steal source maps exposing code structure and business logic, modify alert configurations to suppress security incident notifications, access user session data including IP addresses, user agents, and behavioral patterns, exfiltrate performance monitoring data revealing system architecture, modify project settings and configurations within user's permissions, access integration credentials for services connected to accessible projects, and corrupt historical error data causing loss of debugging information and audit trails for projects within the user's scope.
recommendation: Never hardcode Sentry user authentication tokens in source code, configuration files, scripts, or version control repositories. Immediately revoke the exposed token from your Sentry user settings (User Settings → Auth Tokens → Revoke), generate a new replacement token with minimal required scopes for the specific task (avoid broad permissions, use specific scopes like 'project:read', 'event:read', 'project:releases'), and audit recent API activity in Sentry's audit logs for unauthorized access or data exfiltration. Store tokens securely using environment variables (SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, SENTRY_USER_TOKEN), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager, GitHub Secrets), or encrypted configuration (never commit to Git, Docker images, CI/CD logs). Implement least privilege by creating separate tokens with scoped permissions for different use cases: 'event:read' for monitoring dashboards, 'project:releases' for CI/CD deployment automation, 'event:write' for error reporting tools. Rotate user authentication tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended) and maintain an inventory of active tokens with their purposes. Use Sentry's IP allowlisting if available to restrict token usage to known infrastructure. Monitor Sentry audit logs for unexpected API calls, unauthorized project access, or configuration changes. Enable two-factor authentication (2FA) on your Sentry user account to prevent account takeover. Review and minimize the user's role permissions within the organization to limit the blast radius of token compromise. Configure data scrubbing rules to prevent sensitive information from being captured in error reports. Regularly audit which projects the user has access to and remove unnecessary permissions.
impact: High security risk - Exposed Sentry user authentication tokens grant user-level access enabling: sensitive data exfiltration from error reports containing API keys, database credentials, authentication tokens, and personally identifiable information (PII) leaked through stack traces and context variables from all projects the user can access; manipulation or deletion of error tracking data to conceal malicious activity or disable monitoring within user's scope; access to release information and source maps revealing code structure and application architecture; theft of performance monitoring data exposing system details; modification of alert configurations to suppress security notifications; access to user session data violating privacy; access to integration credentials for connected services; corruption of historical error and performance data causing loss of debugging information and audit trails; and potential privilege escalation if the user account has elevated permissions within the organization.

compliant.description: Using environment variables for Sentry credentials
compliant.code:
import os
import sentry_sdk

# Load Sentry token from environment
SENTRY_USER_TOKEN = os.environ.get('SENTRY_USER_TOKEN')

sentry_sdk.init(auth_token=SENTRY_USER_TOKEN)

checks.message: Sentry user authentication token detected in source code. This token provides user-level API access to Sentry's error tracking and application performance monitoring platform, granting access to projects and resources that the specific user has permissions to view and manage. Exposing this authentication token allows attackers to: access sensitive error data from all projects the user can view including stack traces containing API keys, database credentials, authentication tokens, session cookies, and personally identifiable information (PII) such as email addresses, usernames, IP addresses, and user behavior patterns leaked through error context, custom tags, and breadcrumbs, manipulate or delete error tracking and performance monitoring data within the user's accessible projects to hide malicious activity or cause operational blindness preventing incident detection, access detailed release information revealing application architecture, deployment patterns, version history, and potential security vulnerabilities, steal source maps exposing proprietary code structure, business logic, API endpoints, and internal function names, modify alert rules and notification configurations for accessible projects to suppress security incident alerts, access comprehensive user session data including session replay recordings, user interactions, navigation patterns, form inputs, IP addresses, geolocation data, and device information violating user privacy, exfiltrate performance monitoring data exposing system architecture, database queries, API endpoint response times, and infrastructure bottlenecks, modify project settings and configurations within the user's permission scope potentially introducing security misconfigurations, access integration credentials for third-party services (Slack, Jira, GitHub) connected to accessible projects enabling lateral movement, delete or corrupt historical error data and performance traces causing permanent loss of debugging information and audit trails, enumerate accessible projects to map the application portfolio and identify high-value targets, create malicious releases with backdoored source maps, modify transaction sampling rates to hide malicious patterns, and if the user has elevated permissions (Admin, Manager, Owner roles), perform organization-level operations including team management, billing access, and organization-wide configuration changes. For users with elevated permissions, compromised tokens can result in: complete operational visibility loss across multiple projects; theft of customer data and PII from error contexts violating privacy regulations (GDPR €20M or 4% revenue, CCPA $7.5K/violation, HIPAA $1.5M/category); exposure of credentials leaked in stack traces enabling lateral movement; corruption of compliance audit trails; reputational damage from data breaches; and loss of intellectual property through source map theft. IMMEDIATELY revoke this token from Sentry user settings at https://sentry.io/settings/account/api/auth-tokens/ (User Settings → Auth Tokens → [Token Name] → Revoke), generate a new replacement token with minimal required scopes avoiding broad permissions (use specific scopes: 'project:read' for read-only access, 'event:read' for error data, 'event:write' for creating events, 'project:releases' for release management, and avoid 'org:admin' or organization-wide scopes), audit Sentry's audit logs for unauthorized API activity checking for: bulk data exports, rapid project enumeration, configuration modifications, unexpected API calls from unknown IP addresses, data scrubbing changes, integration modifications, and suspicious patterns indicating reconnaissance, review accessible projects for unauthorized changes to DSN keys, alert rules, release configurations, and integration settings, check for newly created or modified integrations that could be used for data exfiltration, examine error events for unusual patterns such as bulk deletions or artificial injections, verify no malicious source maps were uploaded, review the user's role and permissions within the organization to assess potential impact scope, store authentication tokens securely using environment variables (SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, SENTRY_USER_TOKEN never hardcoded), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager never in repositories), or encrypted configuration (never commit to Git, Docker images, CI/CD logs, or public locations), implement least privilege by creating separate tokens with scoped permissions for different use cases: 'event:read' + 'project:read' for monitoring dashboards, 'project:releases' for CI/CD automation, 'event:admin' for error management (never use broad scopes unless necessary), rotate user authentication tokens regularly every 90 days maintaining inventory of active tokens with creation dates, scopes, purposes, and last usage, enable Sentry's IP allowlisting to restrict token usage to known infrastructure preventing usage from unexpected networks, monitor Sentry audit logs for suspicious activity: unexpected API calls, unauthorized project access, configuration modifications, bulk data exports, API calls from unexpected IPs or geolocations, rapid API calls indicating scanning, and token usage spikes, implement token expiration policies and automated rotation procedures, configure data scrubbing rules to prevent sensitive information capture: scrub environment variables with 'key', 'token', 'secret', 'password', 'api'; remove query parameters and headers with authentication data; mask PII in user context, event tags, and breadcrumbs, enable and enforce two-factor authentication (2FA) on the Sentry user account preventing account takeover even if credentials are compromised, regularly audit the user's role and project permissions removing unnecessary access following principle of least privilege, review error data retention policies to minimize exposure window of sensitive information, configure alerts to detect unusual API activity: bulk exports, rapid enumeration, unauthorized changes, API calls from unexpected regions, usage spikes, and deletion patterns, use team-based access controls to segment project access ensuring tokens only access authorized projects, implement webhook signature verification on integrations, regularly review Sentry security advisories and updates, conduct periodic audits of error data to identify sensitive information captured despite scrubbing, maintain incident response procedures for token compromise including: immediate revocation, audit log analysis, data breach assessment, customer notification if PII exposed, and regulatory reporting if required, use separate Sentry accounts for different environments (dev/staging/prod) preventing development token compromise from affecting production, and implement network segmentation restricting Sentry API access to authorized systems preventing token usage even if exfiltrated from source code.
